data.title: SPEM: Heavy Bet On Emerging Asia Ex. South Korea
data.description:
SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF Opportunity Overview

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF (SPEM) is a relatively unique vehicle that provides stronger exposure to some of the dominant emerging Asian economies, including China, India, and Taiwan.

This approach has led to substantial outperformance in the long run, even amid concerns over China's stock market and economy. If you want concentrated exposure to emerging Asia, this is a favorable vehicle to consider.

However, 2025 performance is also worth noting, as there were many surprise victors in the market, including South Korea and Brazil, which trounced other emerging markets. Herein lies the main downfall of this ETF, as it is not positioned to significantly benefit from rallies in some of the smaller constituent emerging markets.

2026 looks like a solid year for many emerging markets, with key Asian markets like Taiwan and China poised to benefit from increased AI demand. This ETF looks like an acceptable addition to an emerging market portfolio, with notable differentiation from the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, but it still excludes a lot of larger victors and other smaller underdog emerging markets that have a lot of potential this decade.

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF looks like a solid hold at the moment, even though I have some concerns about broader geopolitical and economic risks, as well as valuations in key markets like Taiwan and India.

Portfolio and Benchmark Index Overview

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF is an exchange-traded fund that tracks some of the dominant emerging markets, including China, Taiwan, and India. It charges a 0.07% fee and tracks the S&P Emerging BMI Index.

SPEM has over 3,000 holdings with an average market cap of $159 billion. This ETF is a relatively attractive value play, as it trades at circa 15x earnings and 2x book value, a slight discount to the MSCI EM Index.

Even though it primarily invests in larger companies, the SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF still offers a circa 2.5% dividend yield.

The ETF's benchmark index has some unique features relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, a common benchmark for many emerging market ETFs. The index chooses emerging market companies based on a variety of characteristics, including the market cap size and 6-12 month median trading value.

S&P Emerging BMI Index

This index primarily focuses on information technology and financial companies in leading emerging markets, with China, Taiwan, and India accounting for nearly 75% of the entire index. This index is around 10 percentage points overweight in these markets relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

The SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF efficiently tracks this benchmark index and is consequently a superior bet for those who are more bullish on dominant Asian emerging markets.

As seen below, its main holdings mirror the index, with a maximum of a circa 30 basis points difference relative to the index’s top holdings.

SPEM Top Holdings

This ETF has a slightly higher weighting in IT and financial stocks, which have been some of the top-performing industries in emerging markets. Both of these industries collectively account for 44.2% of its assets.

Heavily focused on Asia, ex. South Korea: Its country approach is also in line with its benchmark index, as its top three constituents account for around 73% of its total assets. It is also underweight markets like Brazil, which make up a large % of the MSCI EM Index. This concentrated approach is good for Asia bulls, as it provides exposure to some of the dominant Asian economies, excluding South Korea, but leaves the ETF vulnerable to regional pullbacks.

Emerging Asia Potential

These markets have ample long-term growth potential, but still lag behind other dominant EM names. This is a weird setup, especially since South Korea and Brazil are top 5 MSCI EM constituents and have a favorable outlook ahead of them.

This underperformance comes at a time when valuations in markets like India and Taiwan have become stretched, while Brazil and South Korea trade at lower valuations.

P/E P/B Dividend Yield
MSCI India Index 26.9 3.8 1.2%
MSCI Taiwan index 23.1 4.1 2.1%
MSCI Brazil Index 9.4 1.7 6.0%
MSCI South Korea Index 17.1 1.6 1.4%

Source: MSCI's latest available data

China's Growth Prospects Are Favorable

China is still well worth considering as a value play, although its higher weighting is a source of concern. The IMF recently upgraded its growth potential for China to 4.8% next year, in line with its 2020-2023 average.

An Irrational Discount to Emerging Markets

At the moment, China is still attractively priced given its dominant standing in AI and other technological innovations. MSCI China still trades at a slight discount to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, despite this standing.

Chinese equities are also trading at a massive discount to US equities, which have become inflated in recent years.

Taiwan's New Record Growth

As AI investments continue to ramp up, Taiwan has positioned its economy in a very favorable way. The country's economic growth is poised to hit a 15-year high on the back of record AI demand.

India Economic Outlook and Valuation Concerns

India, which accounts for nearly 20% of this ETF's assets, has a very favorable outlook, but is one of the pricier emerging markets. However, India still has one of the strongest growth outlooks in the world at the moment, and looks like a solid hold.

Risks and Other Additions to Note

One of the main factors to note is that it is possible for investors to access the market at even lower valuations by avoiding markets like India and Taiwan, and going overweight other markets in Asia and South America. For example, Brazil and South Korea are still compelling value plays at the moment.

SPEM is heavily exposed to some of the economic risks in the Asia region, and particularly to any potential China and Taiwan tensions, given its 50+% weighting in these countries.

This vehicle is still unique from other emerging market funds, and offers reasonable valuation despite its exposure to more expensive Asian markets. It has also been a long-term outperformer of the MSCI EM Index.

I give this ETF a hold rating, especially since Chinese equities seem reasonably priced despite the risk, and it invests circa 30% of its assets in other promising emerging markets. However, I think its choice to exclude other dominant and promising value plays makes other emerging market ETFs better picks.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Dividend Cut Unlikely In 2026
data.description:
My Long Thesis For Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL)

With interest rates falling and many BDCs sensitive to falling interest rates, the risk of dividend cuts is looming over the industry at large. One BDC that I deem to be quite insulated from a dividend cut is Blackstone Secured Lending which has recently seen a contraction in its spread between net investment income and dividend distributions in the last year, but which still covers its dividend at a rate of more than 100% (as of Q3-2025).

Plus, available undistributed spillover income is a buffer against a short-term short-fall of GAAP NII. Blackstone Secured Lending’s BV multiple retreated to about 1.0x lately which culminates in a buy recommendation.

High-Quality Private Credit Pool Facing NII-Related Coverage Challenges

A number of BDCs have cut their dividends in 2025, the latest one being FS KKR Capital (FSK) which is the 5th largest BDC by a market valuation. FS KKR Capital has changed its dividend setup in light of performance challenges and high non-accruals in its private loan business.

The result: it will pay a lower $0.45 dividend in Q1-2026 and add a variable component of $0.10 to the payout. In future quarters, this variable dividend will depend on credit performance. The challenges faced by FS KKR, which had cost-based non-accruals of 5% in Q3-2025, underscores the need for you to pick the best of the best BDCs.

Blackstone Secured Lending is predominantly invested in first-liens and the BDC has earned a reputation amongst investors as a low-risk private credit platform, and for good measure: Blackstone Secured Lending had only 0.1% of its assets marked on non-accrual, meaning only a tiny sliver of its more than $13.8 billion in investments are seen as problematic.

Blackstone Secured Lending, if anything, is the exemplary BDC with an almost sole focus on senior secured debt. In Q3-2025 Blackstone Secured Lending had an almost exclusive investment focus on senior secured loans, making up 97.5% of the private credit pool’s investment allocation. A marginal percentage of assets, only 2.5%, related to investments other than first-liens, which for the most part were lower-ranked second-liens and some equity positions.

Associated with Blackstone Secured Lending’s first-lien profile is what I consider to be the BDC’s biggest vulnerability. In the graphic breakdown of the private loan business’s portfolio you can see that 98.6% of BXSL’s investments are in floating-rate debt. This, for obvious reasons, is a potential problem for the BDC as the market has started to price lower interest rates into asset prices.

In fact, crashing crypto currency prices, for example, indicate decreasing risk appetites and therefore suggest that the market is less willing to gamble on high-yielding credit vehicles. For Blackstone Secured Lending this could become a problem, especially if the BDC were to see a falling spread between its GAAP NII and its distribution.

First-Lien Portfolio, But Contracting NII Spread

Yet, despite a strong asset performance, there are risks for Blackstone Secured Lending and you should not assume that a high-quality BDC could not be forced to lower its payout if its loan performance does not support its payout. Blackstone Secured Lending generated $737 million in NII in Q3-2025 LTM, up 6% YoY, because of new loans made during this period.

Blackstone Secured Lending’s spread (the difference between its GAAP NII and regular distribution) is still positive and averaged $0.06 per share in the last four quarters. Only once in the last year did the dividend coverage ratio fall to 100% (in Q2-2025), but the ratio rebounded to 106.5% in Q3-2025.

The spread in Q3-2025, signalling the BDC’s excess income, was $0.05 in September 2025. The trend since Q124 shows that the degree of coverage nonetheless deteriorated which makes it imperative to look at the business’s spillover income.

Spillover income acts as a reserve account from which a BDC can pull funds down in order to make up a shortfall in GAAP NII. Blackstone Secured Lending’s spillover income in Q3-2025 was $1.89 per share.

This undistributed taxable income is income from prior periods that was retained on the business’s balance sheet. This excess income is a buffer for the BDC to prevent a dividend cut if current-quarter GAAP NII slips below Blackstone Secured Lending’s $0.77 distribution. The undistributed spillover income is equal to 61% of Blackstone Secured Lending’s total FWD dividend payout.

The spread, or safety margin, is narrowing and there is no guarantee that the BDC won’t cut its dividend if, for example, the Fed were to accelerate its rate cuts in 2026.

Fair Value Estimate

I prefer to acquire BDC yields at a decent-sized 10%-or-higher discounts to net asset value, for safety considerations. However, I am willing to pay full price (meaning BVPS) for a BDC’s potential and covered payout if the business has a history of strong asset quality and good loan performance. This is clearly the case with Blackstone Secured Lending which makes the BDC long-term buy-and-hold at 1.01 BV. The average BDC has a 0.9x BV multiple.

My game plan for BXSL looks as follows: I buy BXSL up to BVPS ($27.15) and maintain a buy rating. Below 0.9 BV ($24.44) I would upgrade my rating to strong buy. Above 1.1 BV ($30.00) I would downgrade BXSL to hold only.

The only BDCs that are getting a premium to BV in the market are Blackstone Secured Lending and Ares Capital which mainly relates to strong long-term asset performance, resulting in high trust that the dividend will be maintained.

Lastly, from an asset profile setup, Blackstone Secured Lending has the highest first-lien investment percentage and lowest cost-based non-accrual ratio in the peer group, making it potentially the best high-yield investment among BXSL’s comparables.

Risk Analysis

If Blackstone Secured Lending’s NII were to slip below the business’s 77 cents per share distribution, I would likely start to get a bit more antsy. The trend in coverage, in my opinion, unfortunately is not the best and the spread has declined since Q1-2024, though risen QoQ in Q3-2025.

Though I don’t expect BXSL to cut its dividend in 2026, given its more than sufficient spillover account, there might be a risk for the BDC that originates from other private loan businesses.

If one floating-rate BDC were to cut its dividend, many others may follow in its wake, potentially creating a cascading effect in the BDC sector.

My Verdict: Buy

The current situation for Blackstone Secured Lending does not indicate that a dividend cut is probable.

The private loan business covers its payout fully with NII and the BDC has undistributed spillover income equal to 61% of a full year’s FWD dividend payout. Insufficient NII is currently not a problem, neither is asset quality. The last GAAP NII to distribution spread was positive 6.5 cents and the $1.89 per share in spillover income provides considerable protection.

All of these things lead me to believe that a dividend cut in 2026 is highly unlikely, even under consideration that Blackstone Secured Lending has a high floating-rate percentage of 98.6% and interest rates are set to fall next year.

data.title: BAI: A Higher-Conviction AI Play Built For The Next Market Phase
data.description:
The AI ETFs have generally not been around for decades to check performance across cycles. Current analysis becomes restricted to performance comparisons in unidirectional bull markets since 2023 (with a sharp but short correction in April 2025). When analyzing iShares A.I. Innovation and Tech Active ETF (BAI), I will obviously rely on performance trends, but try to dive into potential repercussions in market types different from today's mega-cap dominated and bull conditions. The fact that BAI is active makes that task harder, because BAI may actively change its structural biases evident now. And of course, the fact remains that the AI secular tailwinds are real, and analyzing for bearish scenarios may be a low probability event.

Overall, BAI does look a decent bet on the AI theme that could do well within a basket of AI ETFs. It does well from a growth ETF perspective in bull markets, but drawdowns look sharper compared to some passive strategies. Within the active plays, BAI has some better alternatives, but has the potential to do well in the right market conditions. Overall, BAI is a buy - investors should expect it to be a higher risk higher returns play within the AI ETF alternatives.

Portfolio Insights and Fund Characteristics

The methodology here is active. It focuses on companies that meet current or forecasted revenues or net income thresholds and are projected to be future market leaders in terms of enablement, development, utilization, and/or deployment of AI technology and products. Since the methodology is active, I have presented the sectoral allocations and the top 10 holdings data below relative to that of CHAT - the AI ETF which has pulled away from the crowd and outperformed significantly since April 2025.

CHAT, with a focus on generative AI bets heavily on tech and communications (almost 94% of the portfolio). BAI has a slightly heavier focus on AI-led disruptions in other sectors (~10%). Pure tech and communications is slightly lower at 90%. BAI leans more heavily towards chips, software and infrastructure (classified as technology), where CHAT is more heavily weighted in the platform companies (Meta, Alphabet) that are leaders in developing and deploying Generative AI models.

For two similar sized portfolios, BAI appears to be the more high conviction and concentrated one. In BAI's case, the higher weightage to Broadcom and the inclusion of Celestica and Fabrinet (AI services/manufacturing/supply chain) confirms its commitment to the services and infrastructure layers, particularly the industrials exposure.

Since BAI categorizes its holdings using the following layers - A.I. power, Accelerated computing, Cloud infrastructure, A.I. models, Data, Data tools/infrastructure software, A.I. applications, A.I. services and solutions - I took a look at the detailed full holdings of both the ETFs and broadly categorized them into these buckets.

CHAT and BAI show similar allocations broadly - the distinct approaches have a marginal variation between portfolios, thematically speaking. CHAT appears more geographically and thematically diversified. It owns chips and memory (SK Hynix, Samsung, TSMC, ASML), GPUs and accelerators, cloud infrastructure (Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Oracle, Dell) and AI models (Meta, Palantir, SenseTime, iFlytek). This balances exposure to different parts of the AI ecosystem. BAI also is spread across, but is relatively more focused on high-powered computing and cloud platforms, with heavier allocations to NVIDIA, Broadcom, and US hyperscalers.

Since BAI is an active strategy, its turnover characteristics are important to discuss too. Compared to CHAT, BAI appears to be more conservative there with a turnover of 56% (compared to CHAT's 92%). This implies agility to adapt to market changes, but also sufficient patience to allocated high conviction bets to play out. The lower turnover also reflects in its lower expense ratio of 0.55% compared to CHAT's 0.75%.

Versus Active Peers

The performance (total returns) show slight underperformance in BAI compared to CHAT. ARKQ is an extreme active end of the active innovation theme and has outperformed significantly in the period of analysis. Between CHAT and BAI, in flat to correcting markets, BAI often outperforms CHAT (although marginally). The bulk of CHAT's recent outperformance has been in a unidirectional bull market since April 2025. A part of this data can be explained by the fact that CHAT's diversified AI play ensures better returns when AI rallies are strong and spill over to names beyond US hyperscalers. The same US mega-cap bias in BAI could turn out to be more defensive in consolidating markets.

Merely looking at empirical data, despite the noted differences depending on market conditions, it looks like CHAT holds up reasonably well even in flat to correcting markets, while outperforming in rallies. A deeper dive into performance attribution reveals far stronger performance in the top 10 in the case of BAI. BAI's top 10 account for ~50% of the portfolio and generated ~60% returns from April. The bottom half of the portfolio (more small and mid cap names) underperformed the top half - contributing only 17% of the 77% returns overall.

For CHAT, the top 10 accounts for ~44% of the portfolio by weight and going by the individual stock returns in the top 10, the heavyweights account for only 42% returns since April. The overall portfolio returns are 97% though. In other words, CHAT's remaining portfolio (primarily smaller caps and mid-caps) outperformed significantly and contributed a healthy 55% of the 97% returns. That is, the overall portfolio saw homogeneous performance.

CHAT’s smaller holdings were on high-conviction bets on the companies that build and power AI hardware and cloud infrastructure. These included specialized AI server providers (Super Micro Computer), AI chip designers (ARM), and emerging AI cloud services (CoreWeave). They were sensitive to momentum plays. When the AI rally took off, speculative capital poured into every stock related to the supply chain ecosystem, not just the large chipmakers. Because CHAT’s small and mid cap holdings were essential, pure-play enablers, they experienced exponential price appreciation (multi-bagger returns) that the larger, more diversified stocks could not match.

Versus Passive

The debate between active and passive is sensitive to market environments too, but I feel agility to react to changing trends are the need of the hour as the AI landscape evolves. Since most passive plays (charted below) are heavily market cap biased, they often will react after market changes are absorbed in share prices. However, the active plays also become higher risk in nature (for the relatively higher growth phases on average). In the April sell-off, BAI crashed much more than most of the passive plays.

Below are some outside alternatives to BAI when it comes to AI investment avenues. QQQ or even XLK are not worse off when adjusted for risk (lower drawdowns). But if pure play AI theme is the bet, BAI has consistently outperformed these diversified and passive broad alternatives.

BAI is a Buy

Despite a stronger empirical performance in alternatives (like CHAT particularly), I find value in BAI. BAI gets large caps "very, very right", even compared to CHAT. This means better ability to navigate downturns or flatter markets or when the AI play starts identifying winners over a broad-based rally at the moment. My outlook is that this selective AI play will be a reality in the second leg of the rally.

CHAT’s smaller, high-momentum holdings are highly volatile and will likely suffer sharp corrections if the AI rally stalls. BAI’s broader, more balanced portfolio, which holds many essential AI service integrators and less-speculative names, is inherently more defensive. In a period of consolidation or a general market downturn, BAI's diversification will act as a buffer, allowing it to hold up better than CHAT. Even if the AI rally continues but rewards chosen "monetization, essentials, reasonably valued" themes over narratives (as I expect), BAI should do very well.

data.title: Impax Global Sustainable Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The portfolio underperformed its FTSE Global Infrastructure benchmark during the quarter. A strong recovery across global equity markets has not benefitted the portfolio’s defensive growth tilt. At a headline level, the portfolio’s positions in companies that are enjoying rapid growth driven by structural trends, such as data centres and electrical grid spending, have performed strongly. Conversely, stocks with more stable growth profiles, such as waste management and water utilities, have given up some of their returns.
  • Company results have also played a significant role in driving performance over the quarter. At a time of relatively elevated valuations and geopolitical uncertainty, even tame earnings updates seem capable of provoking double-digit moves. The portfolio has thus benefitted strongly from positive updates from the likes of KLA and Welltower, while seeing equally negative moves in companies such as Chemed.
  • Stock selection and sector allocation both detracted over the period, with currency effects having a neutral impact. Stock selection was strongest in the Real Estate and Information Technology (IT) sectors. However, this was offset by weaker picks in Industrials and Utilities. In terms of sector allocation, the portfolio benefitted from its overweight to Financials. Overweight positions in Real Estate and Health Care made significantly negative contributions to performance.

Market overview

  • Global equities posted strong gains in Q3 2025, supported by resilient macroeconomic conditions, improving corporate earnings, fiscal stimulus and deregulation in the banking sector. However, market dynamics favoring hypergrowth, cyclical and value stocks have created headwinds for investors focused on quality, and market concentration increased over the period.
  • The passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB) in the US was a key catalyst early in the quarter, providing short-term stimulus through extended tax cuts and incentives for domestic reshoring. While concerns linger over its longer-term impact on the US fiscal deficit and inflation, the OBBB helped lift investor sentiment, particularly in large-cap and technology stocks. The US Federal Reserve’s September interest rate cut also helped to support share prices and extend the risk-on rally in equities.
  • Emerging markets also performed well, buoyed by a weaker US dollar and supportive policy measures in China, which included new renewable energy standards targeting high-emission industries and support for the technology sector.
  • As we end Q3, US regulatory uncertainty has eased for Health Care stocks with the announcement of a deal between President Trump and drug company Pfizer (PFE) to lower prescription drug prices for the government-funded Medicaid program. In the short term, the US government shutdown may weigh on investor sentiment but historically these shutdowns have been resolved with little impact on equity markets.
  • Despite elevated valuations and increased market concentration, the investment team believes opportunities exist in stocks that have been de-rated despite strong earnings momentum and among companies demonstrating strong secular growth. The investment team remains focused on identifying businesses with durable growth drivers, sound management and attractive relative valuations.

Contributors

  • Tele2 (OTCPK:TLTZF)(Communications & Data, Sweden) made the largest positive contribution to performance. Shares in the Swedish telecoms provider rallied after management reported a significant Q2 earnings beat. The company has made faster-than-expected cost reductions under the new Iliad-led management team and is also delivering robust revenue growth in the Baltic region.
  • Welltower (WELL)(Healthcare, US) also boosted portfolio performance. Shares in the healthcare real estate investment trust (REIT) rose following strong quarterly results, notably in senior housing where occupancy and margins were both ahead of expectations. Management is adding capacity through acquisitions and increased the dividend. Successive updates from the company suggest that the pace of expansion is being sustained.
  • KLA (KLAC)(Water, US) further lifted returns. The specialist provider of semiconductor manufacturing tools and services reported results which came in ahead of expectations and saw guidance raised. The latter is notably ahead of competitors. KLA has been a strong beneficiary from continued investment in AI-related technology, with share price performance to match. As a result, we are monitoring its valuation closely.

Detractors

  • American Tower (AMT)(Communications & Data, US) made the largest negative contribution to performance. Shares in the broadcast tower REIT weakened following news that one of its customers – EchoStar – which accounts for roughly 2% of revenue, will terminate agreements after spectrum sales. With the company having recently increased its FY25 guidance, American Tower remains one of the strongest companies in its peer group.
  • Voltronic (Energy, Taiwan) also declined over the quarter. The supplier of power management solutions has been weak on the back of declining demand for its power storage and solar inverter business. On power storage, demand was impacted by tariff concerns and a weak macroeconomic environment. For its solar inverter business, rising competition from China has impacted both growth and margin outlook.
  • Sika (OTCPK:SXYAY)(Buildings & Facilities, Switzerland) similarly detracted from performance. The Swiss supplier of construction chemicals missed sales expectations amid currency pressures and weaker China demand. While local currency growth and margin improvements helped, overall revenue declined. Management lowered full-year guidance, citing macroeconomic uncertainty. Strategic acquisitions and expansions continue, but its near-term outlook is cautious.

GICS sector attribution#

  • The portfolio underperformed its FTSE Global Infrastructure benchmark. Stock selection and sector allocation both detracted over the period, with currency effects having a neutral impact.
  • Stock selection was strongest in the Real Estate and IT sectors. However, this was offset by weaker picks in Industrials and Utilities. In terms of sector allocation, the portfolio benefitted from its overweight to Financials. Overweight positions in Real Estate and Health Care made significant negative contributions to performance.

Outlook

  • Global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index, stood at all-time highs at the end of Q3. President Trump’s onslaught of tariffs has not instigated a massive surge in US inflation, and American consumers remain resilient thus far. US government bond 10-year yields are at 4.1%, down from their peak of 4.8% in January. Even the long-awaited resurgence in M&A appears to have arrived, with 2025 set to be the best year for mergers since 2021.
  • Against this rosy picture must be balanced some less positive data. Seven AI-linked companies now account for over 20% of the ACWI’s market cap. Gold – historically viewed as a safe haven asset – also now trades at all-time highs. From a macroeconomic standpoint, US inflation remains above the Federal Reserve’s target rate. Meanwhile unemployment is ticking up, with a US government shutdown only muddying the picture by preventing the publication of jobs numbers. Globally, yields on long-dated bonds have surged, as investors question long-term fiscal deficits.
  • The managers believe that an allocation to the strategy is more than justified in this environment. Year to date, the portfolio managers have taken advantage of market volatility to initiate positions in companies harnessing long-term structural growth at attractive valuations such as Keppel DC, the Singapore-listed data centre REIT. Market concentration has also enabled the team to buy positions in undervalued defensive compounders, such as US industrial gases group Linde. With an active share of more than 88%, meaningfully higher expected earnings growth than its FTSE Infrastructure benchmark, and a price-to-earnings ((PE)) premium of only 19%, the team is confident that the portfolio is well positioned heading into Q4.

Weighted Average is an average in which each quantity to be averaged is assigned a weight. These weightings determine the relative importance of each quantity on the average.

Return on Equity: The amount of net income returned as a percentage of shareholders’ equity. Return on equity measures a corporations’ profitability by revealing how much profit a company generates with the money shareholders have invested.

∞

The Ex-Ante Beta is calculated using a multi-factor risk model. Beta explains common variations in stock returns due to different stock sensitivities to the market relative to its underlying benchmark for the current period, not historical. A beta for a benchmark is 1.00: A beta greater than 1.00 indicates above average volatility and risk.

†

Dividend Yield, expressed as a percentage, is a financial ratio (dividend/price) that shows how much a company pays out in dividends each year relative to its stock price, does not include return of capital.

*

30-Day SEC Yield: An annualized yield based on the most recent 30- day period.

data.title: A New Market, A New Catalyst: Assessing Hims & Hers' Canadian Opportunity
data.description:
Thesis

The big news this week was that Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) has just taken a step into Canada, via the acquisition of Livewell. It's a move that seems all too similar to the expansion they once made into Europe. I see this as great news for the stock, and I think we will see more upside to come. Hims is viewing Canada as a blank slate in terms of subscribers and should go about gaining customers in much of the same way as they did with Europe. I also see Canadian expansion as something much easier, as there won't be as many language barriers and cultural differences as we saw with Europe. The timing also couldn't be better; the prevalence of obesity is on the rise in Canada, and it is estimated that about 21.2 million Canadians are currently overweight. Hims is also very good at scaling subscriber revenue after they get a good foothold, so I expect them to introduce several of their other offerings into Canada in the near term. The stock is currently trading around $40 per share after the boost in stock price post announcement. I'm quite bullish on Hims shares and expect more upside ahead with the Canadian expansion now on the table.

Two ways to grow

As you know, Hims has just announced its expansion into Canada. In my opinion, it's a big step in the right direction. We’re seeing Hims go down two main roads to boost revenue now that GLP-1s are facing a pullback. The company can either expand their product portfolio, which we’ve seen with testosterone/menopause launches. Or they can expand geographically into new markets. We saw the company earlier this year take a pretty good foothold in Europe, and the easiest way to do that was by acquiring the telehealth company ZAVA. This is exactly what we’re seeing Hims do again, this time by acquiring Livewell, a pretty well-established Canadian digital platform specialising in weight-loss treatment. Hims is now set to enter the market with both strategic momentum and some built-in local credibility. It's also a good time for the acquisition in terms of the demand in Canada. Almost two-thirds of adults in Canada live with overweight or obesity, and access to effective treatment remains quite limited.

So now, with the soon-to-come arrival of generic semaglutide next year, Hims needs to get that foothold as early as they can to set themselves up to scale a lower-cost weight-management program for millions of Canadians. The company is also investing quite heavily in the localised leadership and medical expertise. They’ve just appointed Austin Kouri as General Manager of Canada and brought on Dr. Sandy Van. Dr. Van is actually a recognised obesity specialist set to be Chief Medical Officer, so it goes to show how much focus the move to Canada is about weight loss. Of course, the move will also be supported by the Livewell founders. I think having a Canadian leadership will work much better in their favour in the near term. Telehealth can be a lot different in other countries and to boost that subscriber figure, it's best to get leadership from someone who knows how to address local needs rather than just copying their U.S.-centric model.

A Word On The TAM

Now, I see Canada’s weight-loss opportunity as being quite large, and from the 7% boost in stock price since the post-announcement, the market obviously feels that way too. The direct measures from the Canadian Health Measures Survey show that about 68% of adults aged 18 to 79 are classified as overweight or living with obesity. And if we apply that share to Canada’s 40.29 million population, it gives us roughly 21.2 million adults in that at-risk group. Additionally, the commercial weight-management market in Canada was estimated at $2.43 billion in 2022 and projected to grow to about $4.19 billion by 2030. This would imply an average market spend of roughly $115 per affected adult per year from those who suffer from obesity.

Now, we need to translate that into a practical TAM/SOM view for Hims. If Hims targets the digital weight-management segment, which they’re expected to, and manages to capture 1%, 5%, or 10% of the 21.2 million Canadians with overweight/obesity, that would total roughly 212,000, 1.06 million, or 2.12 million customers, respectively. To put that in context, as of last earnings, Hims had 2.47 million subscribers. Now, by using three illustrative ARPU scenarios, I think we can put a rough estimate on the potential TAM. I’m going to use a market-average of $115, a mid-tier digital program at $300 per year, and finally a high-touch/medication-inclusive program at $1,000 per year. These three capture rates produce annual revenue ranges of approximately $24 million to $212 million at 1% capture, $122 million to $1.06 billion a 5% capture, and $243 million to $2.12 billion at 10% capture. Finally, we have to keep in mind that there may be a much wider availability of lower-cost semaglutide generics beginning in some jurisdictions around 2026 could significantly expand any uptake. It’s set to make pharmacologic treatment more affordable, an important tailwind for telehealth that bundles care and prescription access.

Where I See headwinds

You would have to say that a key headwind for Hims, despite the bullish expansion, would be the margin pressure that comes with their growing dependence on GLP-1 weight-loss treatments. As you know, these drugs are pretty costly, and reimbursement options in Canada and the U.S. remain limited. What also doesn't help is that we're seeing competition from other telehealths or even emerging generics, which might signal a downward trend in prices or subsidies in medication costs. Now, because GLP-1 programs carry higher acquisition costs and thinner margins than many of Hims’ legacy offerings, the push into weight management directly into Canada could slow the company’s path toward stronger profitability in the early days. In effect, weight-loss represents a massive revenue opportunity here, but it also exposes Hims to some cost volatility in a new market and a lot of pricing pressure moving forward. It makes the expansion both a growth engine and a somewhat structural risk.

Valuation

Hims is currently trading at a valuation that clearly implies some strong revenue momentum. However, the company has limited earnings leverage. On a trailing-twelve-month basis, they hold an EV to sales of 4x, roughly in line with the healthcare sector median. But I think the market is valuing Hims mostly on growth rather than profitability. However, its GAAP price-to-earnings ratio is above 70x, and forward estimates still look pretty elevated. It shows us that the stock remains pretty expensive on an earnings basis, and is driven by ongoing investment and modest margins. Despite this, they hold a market cap of $8.4 billion, which is supported by about $630 million in cash and a scalable subscription model. So we should keep in mind that investors are pricing in continued subscriber expansion and substantial long-term operating leverage.

We also have to keep in mind the overall picture of the stock. Back in mid-October, shares were trading just below the $60 mark. And as we saw with many other stocks, the share price saw considerable downside and can now be picked up for $40. However, in that same period, shares reached a low of about $33 and have since started to make a slow recovery. I think the Canadian expansion should help add momentum to that recovery, leading into the next earnings.

Looking Ahead, After The Expansion

Now, in Europe, Hims bought ZAVA to accelerate major European growth. They got access to the UK, Germany, France, and Ireland in one acquisition. ZAVA brought with it an already existing base of about 1.3 million active customers and, in 2024, managed to deliver about 2.3 million consultations across those markets. Back then, it gave HIMS not just scale but also a very mature, functioning digital-health infrastructure that was already laid in place. The local medical teams, pharmacies, regulatory familiarity, customer workflows, delivery systems, and multi-language support were all there for them. So with that foundation, Hims rolled out its much broader suite of offerings in dermatology, mental health, and sexual health into the new geography with relatively low friction.

Next year, I see the same thing happening in Canada; they should just translate that same model via the Livewell acquisition. I think this would give Hims comparable advantages, and perhaps even some that make Canada easier than Europe. First, because Canada shares similarities with the U.S. in things like language, regulatory environment, and lifestyle, Hims can pretty much leverage the U.S. playbook later on, albeit with a few Canadian adaptations, without the complexity of major changes to multiple languages or distinct healthcare-system frameworks as in Europe. Second, the timing right now is very favourable. We’re seeing the anticipated arrival of generic versions of weight-loss medications, and demand for affordable, clinically supported weight-management programs as something likely to surge. Finally, the end goal here would be to offer a unified, digital-first weight-loss and wellness platform combo. Having those already adopting a weight-loss treatment then add a second treatment to the subscription package is what HIMS does best in terms of scaling. I expect that the average subscriber revenue in Canada will grow quite fast. Especially once they reduce barriers around cost, access, privacy, and convenience in Canada. This could even accelerate adoption faster than in some European markets.

data.title: Market-Crushing AI Momentum: Top Robotics Technology Stocks
data.description:
Registration is now open for our Top Stocks for 2026 event with Steven Cress, featuring his newest list of high-conviction picks designed to target long-term performance. Steve’s previous Top Stocks lists have demonstrated an impressive track record, so don’t miss out on this much-anticipated event.

Trump Launches The Genesis Mission: Is Robotics The Next AI Trade?

If the AI trade dominated headlines in 2025, it's about to get a powerful addendum in 2026: Robotics. Designed to make “AI‑accelerated science” a core tool of U.S. national strategy, the Genesis Mission calls for “robotic laboratories” and automated production facilities that can be steered by artificial intelligence.

Washington is moving to back AI’s physical counterpart, with the White House preparing a focused push on robots, automation, and next‑generation manufacturing. A new policy could include an executive order aimed at effectively competing with China, which leads the world with 1.8 million robots deployed. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has been meeting with robotics CEOs and has indicated the White House is “all in” on accelerating the sector.

Nvidia, Fanuc, And Tesla: Robotics In Demand

Robotics are in high demand, and some top companies are capitalizing on this trend across factories, autonomous vehicles, and other technologies. Robotics-related deals and announcements are gaining more investor attention, particularly where they tie into AI narratives with major Magnificent 7 players.

  • NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) Jetson platform is a family of small modules and developer kits that leverage Nvidia GPUs to run AI and robotics workloads directly on edge devices like robots, drones, and smart cameras.

  • Fanuc Corporation (OTCPK:FANUY), a leader in industrial machinery, is partnering with Nvidia to integrate NVDA’s robotic simulation and AI frameworks into its factory software and robot arms, enabling smarter industrial robots.

  • Despite valuation concerns, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) was recently upgraded from Hold to Buy by Seeking Alpha analyst The Techie on the conviction that the company’s Optimus humanoid robot could be a major long-term growth catalyst for the company beyond EVs.

No matter the outcome, robotics‑related deals and announcements are increasingly gaining attention, and the focus below centers on key names.

How I Chose My Top Robotics Technology Stocks

Our universe of AI robotics stocks comes directly from the four largest AI robotics ETFs. These ETFs are managed by professional portfolio managers and analysts whose job is to identify leading companies in the AI robotics space. We take all the securities in those four ETFs and load them into the Seeking Alpha Quant Screener. From there, we rank the stocks using our quant metrics, focusing on companies that score strongly across key investment factors. This is the process we use to identify Quant Strong Buys within the AI robotics category.

My four top AI Robotics stocks are those with the strongest combination of growth and momentum metrics, along with a healthy PEG ratio, my preferred growth stock valuation measure. Using the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, I chose "Sector & Industry" and added each of the five Factors (Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions). I sorted for B or higher momentum and screened for Strong Buys.

1. Fanuc Corporation (FANUC)

  • Market Capitalization: $31.67B.

  • Sector: Industrials.

  • Industry: Industrial Machinery & Supplies & Components.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/4/2025): 23 out of 619.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/4/2025): 2 out of 78.

Fanuc is a Japanese leader in industrial robots, making the arms and control systems that power automated factories globally. It has recently garnered attention after announcing a partnership with Nvidia to use its simulation tools and develop smarter, safer “physical AI” robots for real factory lines. Shares jumped as much as 9% following the announcement of the deal, and the stock has returned more than 14% in the last month alone.

As the No. 2 Quant-ranked Industrial Machinery & Supplies & Components stock showcases exceptional fundamentals, including ‘A+’ Growth. The stock’s solid profitability is powered by an EBITDA margin that's 79% above the sector median, as well as an incredible $4.63 in cash per share.

FANUY Profitability Grade

FANUC offers one avenue for investors to diversify into a well-established non‑U.S. industrial leader, known for its dominant position in robotic arms and factory automation. It’s also important to note that, as with any ADR, FANUC’s U.S.-listed shares are exposed to both currency fluctuations and Japan-specific geopolitical risks. While semiconductors have long been vulnerable to supply chain disruptions and geopolitical risks, that hasn’t stopped Micron from emerging as a standout performer, including as a strong Alpha Pick and a leader within the PRO Quant Portfolio.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Market Capitalization: $262.84B.

  • Sector: Information Technology.

  • Industry: Semiconductors.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of12/4/2025): 1 out of 539.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of12/4/2025): 1 out of 67.

Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) has appeared on several of my recent lists and for good reason. In addition to stunning fundamentals, the stock has showcased exceptional momentum and unanimous positive analyst revisions. As a member of the PRO Quant portfolio, it's up about 77% since its Aug. 8 addition, and it has returned more than 20% in the Alpha Picks portfolio since being added on Oct. 15.

MU ties directly into the robotics theme because its high‑performance memory and storage are essential for AI workloads, where robots and autonomous systems process huge amounts of sensor data in real time. Despite the strong returns, the stock still screens attractively on valuation, creating a compelling GARP (Growth at a Reasonable Price) cocktail.

MU Valuation Grade

The stock’s momentum is indeed “market crushing,” making its valuation shown above all the more attractive. MU has delivered an incredible 97% on a trailing three-month basis and more than 130% over the past year.

MU Momentum Grade

Micron’s core lineup of high‑bandwidth memory and advanced storage is tightly aligned with the powerful secular growth in AI and robotics, where ever‑larger data and models must be moved and processed quickly. The company’s underlying fundamentals suggest it's well-positioned to build on its past success as these trends accelerate.

3. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD)

  • Market Capitalization: $354.26B.

  • Sector: Information Technology.

  • Industry: Semiconductors.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of12/4/2025): 15 out of 539.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of12/4/2025): 5 out of 67.

AMD designs CPUs, GPUs, and other powerful chips that help run AI models in data centers, PCs, and at the “edge,” where robots and autonomous systems operate in real time. Its hardware is especially useful in robotics because it can handle vision, sensing, and control directly on the robot, not just in the cloud. At the company’s 2025 Investor Day conference, Salil Raje, SVP and GM of Adaptive & Embedded Computing Group for AMD, highlighted the company’s opportunity set:

“Billions of intelligent systems will be deployed across all industries in applications such as autonomous driving, robotics and drones over the next decade. These intelligent systems will perceive, decide and act instantly. The physical AI market is expected to be $200 billion plus by 2035. AMD is uniquely positioned to win in this market and gain significant market share.”

The stock showcases exceptional profitability powered by an incredible $6.41B in cash from operations and a TTM EBITDA margin that's nearly 80% above the sector median. In terms of growth metrics, the company notches ‘A’ range grades across nearly every metric. Notably, the EPS FWD long-term growth (3-5Y CAGR) is 173% above the sector median, demonstrating confidence in the company’s long-term trajectory.

AMD Growth Grade

AMD’s valuation has improved from a D three months ago to a C, supported by a forward PEG of just 1.3x (a 24% discount to the sector median). This presents investors with a window of opportunity to benefit from potentially explosive growth as government-backed robotics expansion drives greater demand for AMD’s hardware.

4. Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI)

  • Market Capitalization: $136.24B.

  • Sector: Information Technology.

  • Industry: Semiconductors.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 41 out of 539.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of12/3/2025): 10 out of 67.

Specializing in chips that help machines sense motion, temperature, sound, and pressure, ADI creates components that are used in multiple end markets, including factory equipment and automotives. Analog delivered a strong Q4 FY2025, with revenue rising to about $3.1 billion, up roughly 26% year-over-year, with EPS beating Wall Street estimates. Confidence in its earnings trajectory is reflected in its 26 FY1 up revisions versus just two downward revisions in the last 90 days.

ADI Revisions Grade

The company offers rock-solid profitability, highlighted by its levered free cash flow margin that's 214% above the sector median and $5.10 in cash per share vs. the IT sector’s $2.25.

ADI Profitability Grade

While Analog’s headline valuation leaves something to be desired, it does trade broadly in line with the sector across several key metrics. ADI is a rare tech stock that offers a dividend—while its 1.42% yield is relatively modest, it's backed by solid dividend safety and growth. For income‑minded investors, ADI offers a combination of resilience and growth potential that is relatively uncommon in the sector.

Robotics Could Be the Next Big Trend in 2026

Robotics could emerge as one of the headline investment themes of 2026, especially if Washington follows through with new policy support and executive orders. There's already an active debate about how the Magnificent 7 might plug into the robotics trade and what that could mean for valuations, with Tesla’s Optimus robot even showing up in Kalshi betting markets. Against this policy backdrop, I’ve highlighted four stocks with strong ties to robotics, solid fundamentals, impressive momentum, and Quant Strong Buy ratings: FANUY, MU, AMD, and ADI.

I alluded to Alpha Picks earlier in the article. This service might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "Strong Buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps.

As mentioned above, registration is open for the Top Stocks for 2026 event, building on a strong multi-year track record of top picks that have outperformed the S&P 500. Ahead of the Jan. 6 event, there will be a recap of the 2025 list on Dec. 11, and you can register here.
